evidence_summary: Authors explicitly argue that codebook operationalizations (e.g., CAMEO vs. CCC definitions of 'protest') are load-bearing for measurement validity and that Stage 0 codebook preparation 'cannot be bypassed or automated,' with LLMs failing to respect human-specified construct boundaries.

Authors curate three diverse codebook corpora (CCC, BFRS, Manifestos) as a deliberate pluralist sample and find LLM performance varies systematically by corpus, but the role is less central to the argument than construct-boundary enforcement.

Authors propose a new semi-structured machine-readable codebook format and a five-stage evaluation protocol with behavioral tests; the traditional 'write codebook for human coders' role is redefined to include machine-readable formatting, ablation protocols, and behavioral test design.

The paper introduces a new human labor structure (Stage 0 preparation plus instruction-tuning with human-coded examples) and explicitly allocates which steps cannot be automated, redefining who does what rather than simply performing a pre-AI allocation task.

Authors demonstrate with Test V (exclusion criteria) and ablations that LLMs fail to respect precise construct boundaries (e.g., 'welfare_positive excludes education'), treating human-authored boundary definitions as load-bearing for validity.

The paper introduces seven behavioral tests (Tests I-VII) as new calibration/audit sub-tasks designed explicitly around LLM involvement, redefining what calibration work looks like in a codebook-LLM pipeline.

role_label: Routine coder on high-consensus, ground-truth-rich tasks
evidence_summary: For BFRS after instruction-tuning, Mistral-7B reaches 0.82 F1 relative to human-coded ground truth, indicating AI can match human coders on the higher-consensus subset while residual human labor shifts to training-data curation and error analysis; but Manifestos shows ground-truth-rich tasks are not uniformly captured, qualifying the claim.

Authors explicitly contest the single-ground-truth assumption, treating demographic perspectives as the inferential object; construct boundaries (politeness, offensiveness) are load-bearing and disagreement across groups is 'signal to be preserved, not noise.'

The PDI framework introduces a new protocol (burn-in + adaptive sampling + IPW correction) that redefines annotation protocol around LLM proxies plus adaptive human sampling, a before/after verb swap from 'label everything' to 'sample where LLM error is highest.'

Authors discuss generalization to RLHF, LLM-as-judge, and survey simulation only under the moderator that informative demographic features exist; 'where demographic proxies are weak, the adaptive gains will be smaller, and the method degrades to uniform PPI,' specifying the condition for transportability.

Human annotators from specific demographic groups are treated as load-bearing witnesses to perspective-specific judgments that LLMs (including persona-prompted ones) systematically misrepresent; persona prompting 'is not a substitute for human annotation from that group.'

The paper redefines labor allocation as adaptive budgeting of human annotators to demographic groups with highest LLM error, introducing a new allocation sub-task (error-driven upsampling) that would not be recognizable pre-AI.

Construct boundaries (what counts as polite, offensive) are retained but moderated by demographic perspective; the same construct yields different group-specific means, so boundary-setting is conditional on perspective.

Adaptive perspective sampling is the central contribution; the paper develops an entire methodology around which demographic groups to sample and how much, and lists the framework's validity as depending on faithful perspective representation.
Authors discuss extension to other settings (RLHF, LLM-as-judge) only when informative error-predictor features exist, specifying the moderator under which the approach retains validity.

The paper introduces IPW rectification and bootstrap confidence intervals specifically to calibrate LLM-proxy-based estimates to group means, a new calibration sub-task built around LLM involvement with explicit before/after framing (single-scalar PPI to vector-valued PDI).

Human annotators speaking from their demographic position are treated as essential witnesses whose judgments cannot be faithfully simulated by persona prompting ('fails precisely for the groups it is meant to represent').

Demographic context (gender, age, education) is treated as the axis along which witness testimony varies and whose preservation is the inferential object; chi-squared tests establish that ratings differ significantly by these contexts.

role_label: Routine coder on high-consensus, ground-truth-rich tasks
evidence_summary: Authors explicitly negate the ground-truth premise: 'in subjective tasks, disagreement is constitutive, and we show how these repair methods apply in settings where no single correct label can be defined,' which counter-indicates this role.

Title/abstract screening is the canonical relevance adjudication task, and the paper's central finding is that LLMs can perform it as 'first-screener or triage tool' with up to 100% sensitivity against human ground truth; residual human labor is characterized as full-text screening and verification of model-included records.

Authors use three diverse systematic reviews (VL, AB, COVID-19) and note performance varies by review characteristics (e.g., COVID-19's RCT inclusion yielded higher accuracy due to structured abstracts), specifying corpus-level moderators of LLM adequacy.

The paper proposes a new screening protocol (three-component structured prompt, rating thresholds, majority voting, parallel-vs-series integration) built around LLM involvement, with explicit before/after framing in the discussion of how LLMs should integrate into Cochrane/PRISMA workflows.

Authors explicitly discuss conditions under which findings may or may not transport (small sample of reviews, compact vs. larger models, knowledge cut-off contamination, zero-shot vs. alternative prompting), specifying moderators but retaining a claim of promising generalizability.

The conclusion explicitly redefines the human reviewer's role as 'first-screener/triage' partner or 'second-screener in parallel/series,' with a new sub-task (setting rating thresholds, resolving LLM-human disagreement) introduced as new labor allocation between human and AI.
Authors specify conditions (review topic, structured abstracts, knowledge cut-off) that moderate generalization and note optimal thresholds vary by review and model.

The paper introduces a new calibration sub-task: setting rating thresholds (25/50/75), optimal-threshold estimation via ROC, and majority-voting ensemble, redefined explicitly around LLM output behavior.

Title/abstract screening is a canonical high-consensus, ground-truth-rich task; LLMs achieve 100% sensitivity against full-text-screening ground truth at moderate thresholds, with workload savings up to ~90%, and residual human labor is framed as triage/verification of the narrower included set.

role_label: Routine coder on high-consensus, ground-truth-rich tasks
evidence_summary: Human screening decisions serve as ground truth and LLMs achieve comparable sensitivity with substantial workload savings, fitting the shrinking pattern; the paper notes low inter-reviewer disagreement as a typical condition.

The reproducibility score rubric (1-4) operationalizes a construct whose boundaries were set by a human team (lead author plus 5-member cross-verifier including a legal expert) through consensus; authors treat these boundaries as load-bearing ground truth against which agent performance is measured.

Authors deliberately draw from four sources (mass reproduction, I4R, Retraction Watch, Twitter/X) to balance score distributions and include diverse programming languages and data formats, with explicit analysis of how language/format moderators affect agent performance.

The paper explicitly defines the reproducibility-assessment protocol (standard two-phase procedure per Brodeur et al. 2024) as the load-bearing reference standard that agents must emulate, with limitations framed around agent deviations from protocol steps.

Authors validate generalizability by computing Spearman correlations between paper features and scores (|ρ|<0.1), specifying the moderator under which reproducibility assessments transport across feature variation.

The paper explicitly calls out the cost of human labor (347 social scientists for 110 papers; 5+ years for 100 studies) and frames its contribution around automating this labor; human annotation of ground-truth scores is retained as essential and load-bearing.

The 1-4 reproducibility score rubric with fine-grained distinctions between minor coding errors (score 2) and reporting discrepancies (score 3) is human-authored and treated as the ground-truth construct boundary definition.

Authors check transportability via correlation between paper features and reproducibility scores, finding no meaningful correlations, which specifies the condition under which benchmark results generalize.

REPRO-Bench introduces a new calibration artifact (112-task benchmark with applicability and adjusted accuracy metrics) built specifically around agent involvement, and REPRO-Agent introduces new tool-use calibration procedures.

role_label: Routine coder on high-consensus, ground-truth-rich tasks
evidence_summary: Reproducibility scoring has ground truth (from authoritative reproduction reports) but agent accuracy tops out at 36.6% for the authors' own REPRO-Agent and 21.4% for best existing agent, below random chance; the evidence is contradictory between the task being ground-truth-rich and AI being far from matching humans, so a shrinking pattern cannot yet be coded.
